title: Measure the room with sound
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Build a tiny digital tape measure with the HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor, upload the Arduino sketch, and read live distances in Serial Monitor. The real prize is the method underneath: the sensor measures one thing — how long a sound pulse takes to fly out and echo back — and because sound travels at a known speed, that single time reading becomes a distance in centimetres. Learn that pipeline and you can measure the physical world with a stopwatch.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove diagram showing HC-SR04 VCC connected to 5V, Trig to GPIO 13, Echo to GPIO 14, and GND to ground on the ESP32-S3 GPIO extension board.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: HC-SR04 sensor
parts.explanation: The HC-SR04 is a distance sensor. One round transducer sends a sound pulse above human hearing, and the other listens for the echo. The board measures the echo time and turns it into distance.

parts.name: 4 jumper wires
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

parts.name: Official sketch
parts.explanation: A sketch is the Arduino name for a program. This lesson uses Freenove's Sketch_19.1_Ultrasonic_Ranging.ino so you can focus first on wiring, observation, and the measurement model.

wiring.reason: The board sends a short "start" pulse here.
The sensor returns a timed pulse here.
Gives board and sensor the same zero point.

coachInstructions: Guide one physical step at a time and wait for learner confirmation before proceeding.
Once the circuit is reading, teach the core method: Trig sends a pulse, Echo stays HIGH for the round-trip time, pulseIn measures that time, and the fixed speed of sound converts it to distance.
Make sure the divide-by-two lands: the sound travels to the object and back, so the round trip is halved to get the one-way distance.
If a reading is 0 or frozen, check Trig, Echo, GND, and whether the target is flat and square before touching code.
Preserve a working baseline before suggesting modifications.
Keep the Arduino path primary; MicroPython is optional only if already set up.
steps: Place the HC-SR04 so the two round transducers face outward, away from loose wires.
Connect VCC → 5V, Trig → GPIO 13, Echo → GPIO 14, and GND → GND.
Pause and compare your wiring to the chart before powering or uploading.
Upload the sketch to the ESP32-S3.
Open Serial Monitor and set the baud rate to 115200.
Hold a flat object 10–30 cm in front of the sensor and move it slowly closer and farther.

arduino.explanation: Gives GPIO 13 the name trigPin, so the pin's job stays readable wherever the sketch drives it.
Starts a stopwatch when Echo rises and stops it when Echo falls, returning the sound's round-trip time — the one measurement everything else is built from.
Halves the round trip to get the one-way distance, then scales the microsecond timing down into centimetres.

micropython.explanation: Sets GPIO 13 as the trigger output — the board's side of the ping.
Sets GPIO 14 as the echo input — where the return pulse arrives.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: The sensor never touches the object — it times how long a sound pulse takes to fly out and echo back, and the board turns that time into distance.
theoryModel.formula: distance = speed of sound × echo time ÷ 2

notes.title: Why you divide by two
notes.body: The pulse makes a round trip — sensor to object, then object back to sensor. The raw echo time covers both legs, so halving it leaves the single one-way distance you actually want.

notes.title: Why the speed of sound matters
notes.body: The whole conversion leans on sound moving at a known speed. That speed shifts slightly with air temperature, which is one reason a reading can drift by a little even when nothing has moved.

notes.title: Why flat targets read cleaner
notes.body: A flat surface square to the sensor bounces most of the sound straight back, giving Echo a strong, sharp pulse to time. Curved or soft targets scatter the sound, so the return is weaker and the timing less certain.

test.successCriteria: A good reading changes when you move the object. Small wobble is fine.

firstChecks: Check Trig, Echo, GND, and the target's shape.
Confirm 115200 baud, then the board and port.
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.
Aim straight at a flat object.

challenge: Measure a known distance with a ruler, compare it to the sensor's reading, then swap the flat target for a soft or angled one and watch the echo change.
